title: How Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the world

fullText:
These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals

Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? 
Believe it or not, Pokémon Go is 10 years old this month. The wildly successful smartphone game, which allows you to track down lovable creatures in the real world using GPS and augmented-reality technologies, has reportedly been downloaded 800m times across 150 countries and regions. A trillion Pokémon have been caught so far – and I have been responsible for a modest percentage of that figure.

When the game was first released, my sons and I spent many happy hours wandering the streets and parks of Frome searching for Jigglypuffs and Charizards, and the game’s social element, which lets you take on gym battles with other players in your vicinity, offered my autistic son a route to communicate with other children in a way he’d never been able to before.

My boys have grown up now, but I enjoy the fact that the game is still seducing new players into searching their local shopping centre for Machops. There’s a part of me that never really let go of this compulsion, although it’s now evolved. I have a folder of apps on my phone that I have ready whenever I go out for a walk, or visit an unfamiliar town. Seek is a free app that uses image recognition to help you identify plants, trees and animals, and it lets you collect species badges to celebrate new discoveries. The accuracy can be a little variable, but it has given me a new interest in wild flowers and trees, and, being a nerd, I like to imagine which particular species would win in a Pokémon-style battle. How would red valerian fare against field bindweed? What would be the special attack of the common dog violet?

Another favourite is the Merlin Bird ID app, developed by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology. It lets you identify birdsong wherever you are in the world, and provides a “bird of the day” to look out for. My wife and I use it competitively, sending each other screenshots of unusual birds we’ve heard on our travels. I can now fairly confidently identify at least 10 bird songs, and I have to admit, I do associate certain birds with specific Pokémon. Pikachu? The yellow hammer, of course. Corviknight is the raven. Pidgeots are self-explanatory.

Alongside these, I also love Flightradar24 and MarineTraffic, which track airplane and ship movement respectively. I like to know where things are going and why, and to play Top Trumps with the vehicle data, pitting nearby cargo ships against each other in imagined naval battles based on their size and carrying capacity.

I’m not sure I’d be using any of these apps if its wasn’t for Pokémon Go. It opened up in me an understanding of the natural world as a vast playable space, filled with beautiful little signs and systems. When you explore a new game world, everything you encounter is potentially interesting. You have no filter of expectation – in terms of executive function, it’s like being a toddler again. As you get older, you lose that fascination with the minutiae of the world around you – you go for dramatic vistas, rather than the patches of weeds along roadsides; the dawn chorus over the solitary songbird in your garden. Sometimes you need a little prod to bring that back.

During the summer of 2016, and for a few seasons after, every walk we took became an adventure. Mundane places we knew well suddenly bristled with possibilities, boring landmarks became battlegrounds, strangers became allies. I just took all that and applied it to the real things around us – the birds in the woods near where we live, the weeds in our overgrown garden, the planes overhead. I’m not alone in this. Inspired by Pokémon Go, Brisbane student Kris Norris has created a social discovery game based on leaving messages on payphones around the city. Hydrologists in Colorado used a Pokémon Go-style app to discover and track small streams.

I’ve also discovered other location-based experiences along the way, that I might not have thought about otherwise. The fitness game Zombies, Run! was better for me as a running tool than Couch to 5k; Pikmin Bloom is a delightful floral walking game, and Geocaching, where you track down real-life caches hidden by other players, has tricked me into a few long hikes.

What I have learned, or at least had confirmed, since playing Pokémon Go is that all video games have the potential to open new intellectual doorways – new ways to envisage the world and how it works. No time spent in play is wasted. Wonder is a transferable skill.

Mystery games are having a real moment, but brothers Tom and Adam Vian have been quietly perfecting their Detective Grimoire series for more than a decade. I’ve eagerly awaited The Mermaid Mask, in which the titular detective and his smarter sarcastic sidekick Sally try to solve a locked-room murder aboard a submarine, and it does not disappoint.

I always recommend playing these games with a loved one – not because they’re difficult (the puzzles are about as taxing as you’d want for a cosy evening) but because the fully voice-acted, laugh-out-loud script makes them just as fun to watch (and backseat drive) as to actually play. Jordan Erica Webber

Available on: PC, Switch/Switch 2, PS5
Estimated playtime: 10 hours, best enjoyed over a few evenings

Last year, Paramount signed up to make a Call of Duty movie. Now, the studio has confirmed that it will take place in the Modern Warfare universe – which means contemporary weapons, hi-tech espionage and geopolitical slaughter. But who will play moustached SAS hero Captain Price? There are rumours Pedro Pascal is up for the role, but surely it’s got to be Henry Cavill?!

So now it’s all over, it turns out EA Sports FC correctly predicted the World Cup winner for the fifth year in a row. The development team ran a simulation of every group and knock-out match in the competition, where Spain emerged as the victor. I wonder if it also predicted that England v France would be the most video game-like match of the tournament?

On Saturday, GamesRadar ran a feature interview with senior figures at Xbox, who confirmed the importance of exclusive games to the console. “There has to be a reason for people to buy an Xbox,” said chief content officer Matt Booty, accurately. Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution will be the first titles to be affected by the withdrawal from the multiplatform approach that saw titles such as Forza Horizon 6 and Sea of Thieves head to PS5.

Bub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New York

Apple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own family

Castlevania: Belmont’s Curse – Konami classic rises again from Paris sewers and Joan of Arc is a boss

D&D players raise millions in real-life campaign against ‘corporate elite’

Denshattack! – time to get on board with kickflipping trains | ★★★★☆

Splatoon Raiders – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | ★★☆☆☆

This week, a philosophical question from Richard:

“What genuine life lessons have you learned from games? I often think of Verso’s advice to Lune in Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, that the only voice that should be in your head is your own. Bonus hard mode: not just life lessons from dialogue or other ‘narrative’ parts of a game, but from the gaming action itself. The hours I spent trying to beat the last level of Super Mario Odyssey taught me a lot about my flaws: panicking in the face of new situations, and stumbling over bits I had done umpteen times before because I contemptuously thought I’d mastered them.”

One lesson I took from platform games such as Impossible Mission and Jet Set Willy and later from stealth games, was to stop and observe any complex situation before leaping in. That has saved me a lot of trouble. I’ve also picked up little snippets of practical information that I’ll never get to use in real-life – I discovered the Scandinavian flick playing World Rally Championship (it’s a driving technique, not a haircut) and the Coriolis effect while playing the famed assassination mission in Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare. Finally, as a novelist, I learned plenty of things about comedy writing from The Secret of Monkey Island – specifically the importance of understatement and how to set up a gag and then keep it running. The line “That’s the second-biggest monkey head I’ve ever seen” will always stick in my head in relation to both of those skills. I should have it crocheted and framed on my wall.

If you have a question for Question Block – or anything else to say about the newsletter – email us at pushingbuttons@theguardian.com.

title: Fitness emergency! Nearly a dozen Indian stars called to CoE ahead of Sri Lanka tests
description: Nearly a dozen Indian cricketers, including Mohammed Siraj, Ravindra Jadeja, and Hardik Pandya, undergo mandatory fitness tests at the CoE ahead of the Sri Lanka Test tour.
fullText:
Fitness emergency! Nearly a dozen Indian stars called to CoE ahead of Sri Lanka tests originally appeared on Cricket News. Add Cricket News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Nearly a dozen Indian cricketers set to assemble at CoE this week.

Fitness checks planned before Test tour of Sri Lanka next month.

BCCI wants no further breakdowns ahead of packed 2027 calendar.

There has been a reported uptick in activity at the BCCI's Centre of Excellence in Bengaluru, with the facility gearing up to welcome a large group of contracted and shortlisted players over the coming days.

According to The Times of India, a dozen Indian cricketers are expected to be present at the facility by the end of this week, each there for slightly different reasons.

Several names are already stationed at the centre, including Hardik Pandya, Nitish Kumar Reddy, Varun Chakaravarthy, Harshit Rana, and Akash Deep. They are set to be joined shortly by Washington Sundar and a handful of others as preparations intensify before a gruelling calendar.

The BCCI wants to complete a round of fitness assessments for players in contention for next month's Test tour of Sri Lanka.

Mohammed Siraj is due to arrive at the facility this week, with Ravindra Jadeja potentially following soon after. Meanwhile, several targeted players, including Sarfaraz Khan, Musheer Khan, and Abhishek Porel, have already completed their evaluations, and staff at the centre anticipate even more arrivals over the coming days, particularly before these cricketers head off to represent their respective zonal sides in the Duleep Trophy.

MORE: No update in fitness SOP since 2023: BCCI CoE under the scanner after fitness issues in India camp

The BCCI is determined not to leave anything to chance regarding player fitness ahead of the Sri Lanka Test series, with comprehensive assessments planned before selectors finalise their squad for the two-match series.

"Hardik has been there training, Reddy is recovering, and Varun and Harshit are undergoing their rehabilitation," a source told ToI.

"The team management doesn't want to take any chances with regard to its players ahead of the Sri Lanka Test series.
Thorough assessments will be carried out, and the selectors will be informed about their status before the team is picked for the two Tests. The CoE has a fair idea of the targeted players after the red-ball tournament in Bengaluru."

India continue to grapple with fitness concerns as they build toward the 2027 World Cup. Naturally, the last thing the setup wants is further injury breakdowns either before or during the crucial upcoming Test series.

Most centrally contracted players are staring down an extremely packed schedule ahead, spanning all three formats, including an extended tour of New Zealand comprising five T20Is, five ODIs, and two Tests.

Taking this into consideration, the management is keen on maintaining a wide, fully assessed pool of players ready to be called upon, since multiple squads will likely need to be fielded simultaneously across different assignments in the months ahead.

MORE: Tilak Varma reveals how playing mostly in Asia exposed India's batting struggles in T20I series

This proactive round of fitness testing feels like a long-overdue correction rather than routine practice, especially after a year littered with hamstring strains, quad injuries, and rushed comebacks.

Gathering nearly a dozen players together for thorough assessment before a Test series, rather than scrambling after an injury strikes mid-tour, signals the board finally treating fitness as a planning priority instead of an afterthought. What will matter most going forward, though, is whether this becomes a consistent practice tied to every major assignment, rather than a one-off response triggered by recent embarrassment in England.

With a brutal, multi-format calendar stretching well into the World Cup year, India simply cannot afford another wave of last-minute withdrawals. This week's activity at the CoE looks like a genuine step toward accountability, but only sustained follow-through will prove whether real lessons have actually been learned.

title: Fitness influencer Clavicular says he started taking testosterone at 14, calls natural training 'a waste'
description: Fitness influencer Clavicular told Sean Hannity he secretly ordered testosterone at age 14, calling natural weightlifting a "complete waste of time."
fullText:
Clavicular revealed on the "Hang Out with Sean Hannity" podcast that he abandoned natural weightlifting as a teenager in favor of taking testosterone.

On Tuesday's episode, the fitness and looksmaxxing personality – known for his controversial focus on "looksmaxxing" – called traditional training without performance-enhancing drugs a "complete waste of time."

Clavicular, whose real name is Braden Eric Peters, told Hannity that his interest in fitness began in his family's basement gym in Hoboken, New Jersey. After researching workout routines online, he decided that making progress naturally was too slow.

Low Testosterone? Hegseth Sparks Conversation As Doctors Reveal Warning Signs Every Man Should Know

"I started reading different articles and forum posts about how to properly train as one would being a beginner to the gym, and that's sort of where I came to the conclusion that there's no reason to train naturally," Clavicular said.

"It's a complete waste of my time when I have this mechanism I could utilize, which was testosterone, to make the results in a much more efficient timeline. So why wouldn't I?"

The decision led Clavicular to secretly acquire performance-enhancing drugs at a young age, he shared.

"I used to have to camp out by the mailbox and pretend to be sick from school," he said, adding that he intercepted shipments of testosterone at age 14 to hide them from his parents.

Click Here For More Health Stories

Hannity, a father of two, pointed out that many parents would react with anger and concern, prompting Clavicular to admit that his parents disciplined him repeatedly throughout his teenage years.

"Throughout high school, if I would do a f--- up, I would get sent [to my grandmother's]," he said. "Then, after I got expelled from university for peptides and 'roids, that's where I got sent off to."

Clavicular said his academic career came to an abrupt halt after his public social media posts about his steroid use caught up with him.

"Throughout my entire life, I've kind of been documenting everything in a pretty public way," he said. "I was talking about different cycles I was running, and then someone just sent the forum posts to my university, and it was done."

While public discussions surrounding low testosterone levels and hormone health have grown in recent years – often focusing on symptoms like fatigue, low libido and loss of muscle mass – endocrinologists and medical experts consistently warn against nonmedical androgen use.

In adolescents and young adults, unsupervised anabolic steroid and peptide usage can carry severe health risks, including stunted skeletal growth, premature cardiovascular strain, liver damage, psychiatric mood alterations and irreversible fertility suppression, according to the Endocrine Society.

Test Yourself With Our Latest Lifestyle Quiz

Medical professionals emphasize that while hormone therapy can be beneficial for men with legitimate medical deficiencies, using testosterone as a shortcut for muscle growth bypasses essential metabolic and biological safeguards.

title: DeAndre Hopkins offers high praise for Ravens rookie Ja’Kobi Lane
description: DeAndre Hopkins praised Ravens rookie Ja’Kobi Lane after a workout clip, saying the former USC wide receiver is a playmaker.
fullText:
Ja’Kobi Lane has yet to play a regular-season snap for the Baltimore Ravens, but the rookie wide receiver is already drawing attention from one of the NFL’s most accomplished pass catchers. After a recent workout clip of the former USC standout surfaced, DeAndre Hopkins offered a strong endorsement of Lane’s skill set and potential fit with Lamar Jackson.

“He a playmaker!” Hopkins wrote. “Ball in the air it’s his. Looking forward to see him w Lamar.”

That is exactly the type of praise the Ravens want to hear about Lane entering training camp. Baltimore selected the 6-foot-4 wide receiver to add size, catch radius and red-zone ability to a passing game that already features Zay Flowers, Rashod Bateman and Mark Andrews. Lane does not need to become the Ravens’ No. 1 receiver immediately, but his ability to win at the catch point could give Jackson a target profile the offense has needed.

He a playmaker! Ball in the air it’s his. Looking forward to see him w Lamar https://t.co/3jElBS7yHp

— Deandre Hopkins (@DeAndreHopkins) July 20, 2026

Lane’s appeal is not difficult to understand. At USC, he made his mark as a big-bodied receiver who could win through contact, finish contested catches and create problems near the goal line. That skill set matters in Baltimore, where Jackson’s mobility extends plays and forces defensive backs to cover longer than they want.

Hopkins’ comment also carries weight because of the player delivering it. Few receivers in recent NFL history have been better at attacking the football in the air, winning through tight coverage and turning imperfect throws into completions. If Hopkins sees those traits in Lane, it is worth noting.

The Ravens still have a deep wide receiver competition to sort through. Flowers is locked in as the top option, Bateman enters camp with a chance to solidify his role, and Elijah Sarratt gives Baltimore another rookie receiver with intriguing tools. Devontez Walker is also pushing for a larger role after flashing in limited opportunities.

Lane, however, brings something different. He gives the Ravens size and a natural red-zone target who can help Jackson when windows get tighter. Training camp will determine how quickly Lane can earn trust, handle the playbook and separate against NFL defensive backs. For now, Hopkins’ praise adds another layer of intrigue to one of Baltimore’s most fascinating rookies.

If Lane becomes the type of “ball in the air” target Hopkins described, the Ravens may have found a young playmaker who can give Jackson a new weapon in high-leverage moments.

This article originally appeared on Ravens Wire: DeAndre Hopkins offers high praise for Ravens rookie Ja’Kobi Lane

title: How Sore Is Too Sore After a Workout?
description: Rhabdo is scary and serious, but most likely it's just normal DOMS.
fullText:
It’s normal to feel sore after your first day of a new workout, but maybe you’ve heard horror stories of people who ended up in the hospital after they destroyed themselves with a super intense workout. Chances are, your soreness is normal, but here’s how to know.

DOMS, or delayed onset muscle soreness, usually shows up the day after a hard workout. The more severe it is, the longer it will last, often peaking 48 to 72 hours after the workout. A mild case will just leave you feeling a little sore for a day or two; a rougher one might have you wincing when you walk down stairs for most of a week.

The feeling can range from a dull ache to a burning pain. It will feel the most uncomfortable when you’re contracting the sore muscle, especially if you’ve been sitting still for a while. You might wake up thinking oh god, everything hurts, but once you get up and walk around, you’ll probably feel a bit better.

If you go back to the gym during this time—which you should!—you’ll find that you can still do the same exercises but might find that the soreness makes it harder to do them with as much weight or for as many reps as the first time around.

As a bonus, though, a bout of soreness protects you from getting sore the next time. It’s called the “delayed bout effect,” and you can take advantage of it by showing up to your next workout even if you’re not feeling totally back to normal. DOMS only crops up after new or extremely intense workouts, so once your body is used to a particular exercise, you won’t get a serious case of DOMS anytime soon.

There is an extreme type of muscle soreness that can cause serious health complications, and it’s called rhabdomyolysis, or “rhabdo” for short. In rhabdo, you have so much damage to your muscles that your body, and especially your kidneys, can’t handle the resulting metabolic waste products that end up in your bloodstream.

That said, it’s not very common. If you just did a new workout and you feel sore all over, it’s far more likely that you have a bad case of DOMS. To give you an idea of how rare, this study found 29 cases over a period of four years (2010-2014) in one of New York City’s hospitals. Since it’s just one hospital, there must have been more cases in the city in those years than just 29; but on the other hand, that’s still a teeny-tiny fraction of people who exercise.

But rhabdo does happen. And because it happens more frequently in absolute beginners who go too hard than in experienced athletes (who presumably know when to stop pushing themselves), you may be facing your first bout of extreme soreness and not knowing whether you’re unusually sore or not.

Exercise-induced rhabdo—which, again, is rare—is more likely to occur in people who:

Do an extreme intensity or amount (or both) of exercise in one workout.

Are new to exercise and push themselves really hard.

Are taking certain medications, including ibuprofen or other NSAIDS.

Besides hard workouts, rhabdomyolysis can also occur as a complication from injuries that damage a lot of muscle tissue, and from some medications or other health conditions. The symptoms to watch out for, according to the CDC, can happen anytime in the days following the workout and include:

Severe muscle pain, weakness, and swelling.

Dark urine (“tea- or cola-colored”).

If you have these symptoms, seek medical care right away. That may mean going to the emergency room or urgent care. Once you’re there, a blood test can confirm whether you have rhabdo, and you can get the care you need to make sure you don’t end up with kidney damage or other serious problems. The CDC says to make sure you are getting tests for creatine kinase or creatine phosphokinase, ideally repeated over the course of hours or days.

Besides making sure not to skip your next workout, we have a few tips for you. The bad news is that nothing will make DOMS go away; you have to recover. And to recover, you need sleep and food and time.

But while you’re waiting it out, a few things might help you feel better. Moving around helps, so go for a walk or consider a light cardio workout. Massage can feel good, so get someone to massage you or do some self-massage with a foam roller or a lacrosse ball.

Some people find stretching helps, but make sure not to stretch too hard, which can damage your muscles further. I like to take a hot bath or use a heating pad. (Driving in a car with heated seats is a delight after a workout that uses your back and hamstrings.) And, as always, seek medical care for anything that feels particularly concerning. An article on the internet can’t tell you whether you’re seriously injured or just a little sore, but an actual doctor can.

title: Watch Victor Oladipo's private workout for NBA teams in Las Vegas
description: Former Pacer and Hoosier Victor Oladipo hosts private workout as he looks to return to NBA.
fullText:
Former Indiana Pacers' and Indiana Hoosiers' guard Victor Oladipo recently held a private workout in front of NBA staffers from across the league in Las Vegas. Oladipo has not played in the NBA since 2023 with the Miami Heat after suffering a left-knee injury.

Oladipo was the No. 2 pick in the 2013 NBA Draft after spending just two seasons under Tom Crean in Bloomington. After starting his career in Orlando, Oladipo spent one season in Oklahoma City before he was part of the Paul George trade that brought himself and Domantas Sabonis to Indiana.

Victor Oladipo put on a show at his private workout for NBA scouts & GMs in Las Vegas.

His jumper is still so smooth. pic.twitter.com/unuWxZhNjW

— Legion Hoops (@LegionHoops) July 15, 2026

During his time with the Pacers, Oladipo rose to prominence and reached the peak of his professional career. In Indiana, he was selected to two All-Star games (2018, 2019), and won the NBA's Most Improved Player Award following the 2018 season.

A trade to the Rockets gave Oladipo a brief, 20-game stint in Houston before he moved on to Miami. After appearing in just 42 regular-season games, his season was cut short with an injury. Oladipo returned to the court with the Guangzhou Loong-Lions of the CBA in 2024-25. This past season he returned to the United States, and spent time in the G-League with the Wisconsin Herd and Cleveland Charge. He averaged 13.5 points, 4.6 rebounds, 3.8 assists and 2.1 steals while shooting 38.9% overall and 32.9% on 3s in 15 games with the Herd.

This article originally appeared on Indianapolis Star: Watch Victor Oladipo's private workout for NBA teams in Las Vegas

title: Five Workout Headphone Hacks Everyone Should Know
description: If my AirPods roll under the leg press one more time, I'm going to scream.
fullText:
You can do all the right research and pick out the highest-reviewed, best-sounding, most durable headphones for exercise—the thing is, once you’re working up a sweat, you’ll inevitably find something about your headphones that just isn’t perfect.

Maybe you share my love of the bone-conducting Shokz OpenRun Pro 2, or perhaps you want noise-cancelling earbuds, and you go with my colleague Beth’s favorites, the Powerbeats Pro 2. But once you get them on, you might notice they don’t stay in place during heavy cardio, or the sound isn’t as deep as you need for an intense lift. Before you throw in the towel on your earbuds of choice, consider these small, nerdy adjustments to enhance your audio experience before your next workout.

Stock silicone tips are designed to fit the average ear, which means there's a decent chance they're not the right option for you. If your earbuds ever feel like they're working their way out mid-workout, this is very likely a fit problem.

Memory foam tips like these solve two problems at once: they mold to your ear canal for a passive noise seal, and that same seal creates enough friction to keep earbuds locked in during movement. I recommend looking at the brand of your earbuds and buying straight from the source. For instance, Apple lets you buy new ear tips for your AirPods for around $10.

There are plenty of DIY fixes online, but unless you’re particularly crafty, they might be more trouble than they’re worth. The adhesive might give out the moment you actually sweat, which can leave residue on the earbud housing. A $15-dollar pair of silicone ear hooks (like these) does the same job properly: they clip onto the earbud body and hook around the outer ear, and they're designed to survive a sweaty run without peeling off.

Of course, you can save time and simply opt for earbuds with built-in ear hook designs. I’m currently writing an in-depth review of the Suunto Spark open-ear headphones, but I’ll go ahead and give an early recommendation for their ear hook design right here.

Most people leave their EQ on whatever setting they use for podcasts or the office, which might be poorly suited for a workout. A track that sounds perfectly balanced at rest can sound thin and tinny halfway through a hard interval.

The general move for a workout is a mild bass boost and a slight treble lift to punch through ambient noise (traffic, gym equipment, your own breathing). Everyone's preferences are different, so mess around with it before your next workout. From there, most companion apps—like Shokz, Bose, Beats, Jabra, and more—let you save this as a custom profile so you're not re-adjusting it every session. If your headphones support saved EQ profiles, it's worth setting one up specifically labeled "workout" so you're always one tap away from it.

Bonus hack, since we're already talking audio: if you use podcasts to pace easy workouts, try speeding them up slightly, to around 1.25x. It's a small enough change that the content still sounds natural, but the very slightly faster speech cadence has a way of nudging your stride turnover up without you consciously trying to run faster. For me, it's a cheap, free alternative to a metronome app.

If you have a pair of headphones you use exclusively for workouts, you can set up your phone to automatically do things the moment those headphones connect. On iPhone, this lives in the Shortcuts app under Automation. Create a new automation, choose Bluetooth as the trigger, select your workout headphones specifically (not "any device"), and set it to run when they're connected. From there you can chain in an action—like opening Strava—the second your running headphones pair.

The same trick works for call management. If you don't want to be interrupted by a phone call mid-run, build a second automation that turns on Do Not Disturb (or a custom Focus) when your workout headphones connect, and switches it back off when they disconnect. It's a nice way to really disconnect and focus on your breath. If you're worried about missing something urgent, you can still allow calls from favorites to break through DnD.

IPX ratings tell you your earbuds can survive sweat; they don't tell you sweat won't slowly degrade them over time. If your earbuds ever get properly wet (not just sweaty), most manufacturers recommend wiping them down with a soft, dry, lint-free cloth. Then, make sure you actually let them air dry for a couple of hours before using them or putting them back in the case.

Where you can actually extend their lifespan further is the case itself. Any soft case is basically decorative—it does nothing against a water bottle or dumbbell landing on top of your gym bag. A rugged hard-shell case, like this one for AirPods, is one of the cheapest upgrades you can make.

If you're still running with wired headphones—and I know plenty of dedicated runners who are—cable routing matters. Most people let the cord hang straight down the front of the chest, which means the sweat rolling off your collarbone might run straight down the wire toward the connector at the earbud. That connection point is where corrosion starts, which could eventually kill your wired headphones.

The fix: loop the cable up and behind your ears first, then let it drop down the back of your neck. Gravity pulls sweat down your back instead of pooling at the jack. It also stops the cable from bouncing against your chest with every stride, which is a nice bonus.

title: Guehi and Stones set to start for England in World Cup quarter-final
description: Marc Guehi has won his fitness race and is expected to be fit for England's World Cup quarter-final against Norway, with John Stones set to play alongside the central defender.
fullText:
Marc Guehi has won his fitness race and is expected to start England's World Cup quarter-final against Norway alongside a recalled John Stones in central defence.

Manchester City defender Guehi suffered a minor hamstring strain in the win over Mexico and has missed training sessions this week.

But he is set to be cleared to continue in the heart of England's defence.

He is expected to be playing alongside Stones in defence, who is set to be recalled into the starting XI for the first time since the opening game against Croatia.

Ezri Konsa is set to be moved from centre-back to right-back.

Declan Rice is fit to start despite a sickness bug that has hampered his involvement in the preparations for the game while Noni Madueke is expected to play ahead of Bukayo Saka in another change to the XI.

Stones, 32, has been a substitute in all the subsequent matches since Croatia. However, he played a key role against Mexico, coming on for Saka on 57 minutes following Jarell Quansah's red card.

Expected England team: Pickford, Konsa, Guehi, Stones, O'Reilly, Anderson, Rice, Madueke, Gordon, Bellingham, Kane.

title: “But right now” Saka drops huge fitness update ahead of Norway match
description: Bukayo Saka has confirmed that he is in excellent condition as England prepare to face Norway in the World Cup quarter-final this weekend.Norway have impressed throughout the competition and shocked m...
fullText:
Bukayo Saka has confirmed that he is in excellent condition as England prepare to face Norway in the World Cup quarter-final this weekend.

Norway have impressed throughout the competition and shocked many observers by eliminating Brazil in the previous round. However, England are also enjoying a strong run after defeating co-hosts Mexico in their last match.

The Three Lions are among the leading teams remaining in the tournament, and many supporters will expect them to overcome Norway. However, underestimating their opponents could prove costly, with the Norwegians possessing several world-class players.

Norway have Erling Haaland and Martin Odegaard among their leading stars, meaning England will need their best players available for the important quarter-final fixture.

Saka remains one of England’s most dependable performers, but the winger’s fitness has been carefully managed in recent weeks. As a result, he has not started many matches during the tournament.

Ahead of the meeting with Norway, Saka has provided a positive update on his condition. According to Independent Sport, he said:

“Of course, I would have loved to come to this tournament 100 per cent, but that wasn’t the case and everyone’s realised that. They’ve managed me in the best way possible. But right now, I’m feeling great and I’m ready to go.”

Saka’s availability will be a major boost for England as they prepare for a challenging encounter. His attacking ability and experience make him one of the players who can influence the outcome of the match.

Although England enter the fixture with confidence, they know Norway have already demonstrated that they can compete against the strongest teams in the world. The quarter-final is expected to be a difficult contest, and England will need a complete performance to progress.

With Saka feeling ready and the squad preparing for the challenge, England will hope their key players can deliver when they face a dangerous Norway side.

title: Parents sue fitness club daycare after video appears to show toddler thrown by worker
description: Parents sue after video appears to show toddler thrown by worker.
fullText:
A California family has filed a lawsuit against a Los Angeles-area fitness club after surveillance video appears to show a childcare worker throwing their 23-month-old son over her head.

The incident occurred March 17, 2025, at the Bay Club in El Segundo, California, and is now the subject of ongoing litigation.

According to the lawsuit, the toddler suffered a traumatic brain injury after falling onto a hardwood floor while in the care of a Bay Club employee.

Day care workers who used horror mask to scare children charged with felony child abuse

Surveillance video reviewed by ABC News appears to show the toddler, identified in court documents as C.K., approaching a childcare worker and reaching for her hands.

The worker then appears to swing the child between her legs before lifting him over her head.

The complaint alleges the employee "released C.K.'s hands while he was above the employee's head," leaving him "approximately 6 feet above the ground" before he fell. The lawsuit alleges the toddler hit the hardwood floor before the employee fell on top of him.

The complaint alleges the Bay Club falsely told the parents an employee "fell over while she was in a squatting position" and that the toddler was "only about 1.5 feet above the ground" when he fell, calling that account "a complete lie."

It further alleges the surveillance footage "proves that the Bay Club's description of the incident was intentionally false and misleading."

According to the lawsuit, the toddler was taken to the emergency room later that day for evaluation of blunt head trauma and was diagnosed with a concussion, traumatic brain injury and facial abrasions.

The lawsuit also alleges he continues to experience symptoms more than a year later, including hearing loss.

While gym daycares are typically exempt from California childcare licensing rules, since parents are required to stay onsite, the complaint argues that the Bay Club should have been licensed by California Department of Social Services, alleging that parents are permitted to leave the premises to go to the nearby Manhattan Country Club.

According to the lawsuit, the child’s father checked him into the Clubhouse before going to the nearby Manhattan Country Club, about a mile away, while his son remained in Bay Club’s care.

4 day care workers arrested for lacing children's food with melatonin: Police

"The deception by the Bay Club of hiding this horrific incident from the parents is inexcusable. The day care facility should have the highest amount of care for the children, and if a child gets sick or injured, they should notify the parents with transparency and urgency," attorney Ryan Saba of Rosen Saba LLP said in a statement.

In a statement to ABC News on Tuesday, the Bay Club said, "We are unable to comment on ongoing litigation. At the Bay Club, the safety of our members, team members, and the families we serve is our highest priority."

title: 5 Hacks Every Suunto Run User Should Know
description: Useful tips for this straightforward fitness watch—including a fix for U.S. users' biggest pet peeve.
fullText:
When I reviewed the Suunto Run, I found it to be a delightful little watch. It's lightweight, with a big, bright screen, and it does a lot of what an entry-level Garmin can do, but at a much better price. It's easily one of the best affordable running watches out there, normally costing around $200. Here are some of the best tips and tricks I've found for getting the most out of the watch—starting with a hard-to-find fix for one of its most frustrating default features.

Even if you set your units to Imperial (miles and so on), the Suunto Run will mark a lap and beep at you after every kilometer. When I first reviewed it, I searched in vain for a setting that would let me switch it to miles, or at least turn off the auto-laps. I couldn't find one. Judging from the number of people asking this question on Suunto forums and on Reddit, I'm not the only one who has this problem. Supposedly, autolap can be turned off in settings, but I checked Exercise Options and didn't see it. I just resigned myself to the kilometer beeps. (Five miles is eight kilometers, for example—a fact I'm now quite familiar with.)

But it turns out there is a setting to change the autolap. You can find this either within the app or on the watch itself, but either way, the trick is that you have to choose a type of exercise (like Run or Trail Run) and then, instead of going into Exercise Options, select Free Training. This is the mode you'll normally use when you run, and that's where you'll find Autolap, which is set by default to 0.62 miles. Change that to 1.00 miles or turn it off, as you prefer. You'll have to go into each activity separately to change the autolap. I wish there were a more general setting for it, but at least there's a workaround.

Every watch has a stopwatch feature, but it's often not easy to get to (or requires you to program and remember a button shortcut). Suunto does something special, though: you can put a stopwatch right into your widget list. This way, all you have to do is swipe up from the bottom of the watch. These widgets are where you would normally check how many steps you've taken today, or how you slept, or what the weather is or when you can expect the sun to set. But you can also add a stopwatch, and I recommend that you do.

When you're looking at your widgets, click the top button (it will have a pencil icon). Here you can add new widgets to the list, or rearrange them. Add the stopwatch, and do yourself a favor and put it right at the top. Now you can start the stopwatch with just a quick swipe-and-tap from the watch face. Best of all, you can exit the stopwatch screen, and the next time you pull up your widgets you'll see the stopwatch's time displayed live on its widget. It's the handiest little thing.

This is another feature that most running watches have somewhere, but Suunto makes it so easy to access that you'll end up using it all the time. If you're running on the road (activity type Run) and decide you're going to finish your run on the trails (activity type Trail Run), you can switch between them by long pressing the top button. Once you select the new sport, your run is paused and the watch shows how long you've been paused at this "transition." Hit the start button when you're ready to go, and Suunto will record the two segments as different parts of the same workout.

This is handy because Suunto offers so many different activity types with slightly different data screens and settings. If your trail run turns hilly, you might prefer the Vertical Running activity to get more data on your power zones as you climb. Or maybe you'd prefer to just change to a Hike, something I've done a few times in this miserable heat wave. You can also create custom activities if you want even more control over your data screens and settings.

One interesting feature of many Suunto watches is the ability for the watch to alert you when it detects that a storm might be coming. This is possible because the watch has a barometer/altimeter that measures air pressure. When the weather is constant, changes in pressure hint that you're climbing up or down hills. But if pressure drops dramatically, that can indicate that a storm is on its way. To turn on storm alerts, go into Settings and then Outdoor and turn on Storm alarms.

While you're in this menu, you may want to make sure your barometer is calibrated. Also under the Outdoor menu, you can select Alti & Baro, and then you have the option to either Set altitude or Auto adjust altitude. The auto-adjustment uses a GPS reading to make sure the altimeter is calibrated. But if you know your current altitude from a map, you can set it manually.

Suunto makes it surprisingly easy to save points of interest and navigate to them without a lot of fuss. I use these "POIs," as the watch calls them, to mark interesting or useful areas in my local park. We're blessed with dozens of miles of trails in a small area, and I can't keep everything in my head. For example, I mark water taps so I can find them the next time my hydration pack is running low. I also like to mark places that I might like to return to on a future trip—say if I see a spot I think my kid would like to hike to.

Suunto's library of POI types is broader than I've seen in other watch brands. Besides the obvious—water, campsite, where you parked your car—you could also do some pretty detailed scouting for hunting, fishing, foraging, or wildlife watching. There are built-in POI types for noting where a deer seemed to have rubbed its antlers, where you've seen marine mammals, or where you've spotted mushrooms. You can save your current location as a POI from the watch or plan a trip before you leave by saving POIs on the map in the Suunto phone app.

I used this feature today to navigate to a favorite scenic overlook. You just have to plug in the POI as a Navigation Target under the options for your trail run or other activity. There's also a navigation app, accessible from the bottom of the widget list (select All Apps), and you can navigate without having an activity running.

title: One year after dramatic weight loss, Sarfaraz Khan shows off another stunning fitness transformation in 2026
description: Discover how Indian batter Sarfaraz Khan sustained his incredible 17kg weight loss to unveil another stunning physical transformation during the 2026 season.
fullText:
One year after dramatic weight loss, Sarfaraz Khan shows off another stunning fitness transformation in 2026 originally appeared on Cricket News. Add Cricket News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Sarfaraz Khan stuns fans with another remarkable fitness transformation a year later.

He lost 17 kg after being dropped from India's England Test squad in 2025.

Despite CSK debut and domestic brilliance, IPL 2026 ended with modest returns.

A year after turning heads with a dramatic body overhaul, Sarfaraz Khan has done it again, sharing images on Instagram that show a visibly leaner and more muscular physique, complete with defined abs and sculpted arms that tell the story of months of relentless discipline.

The original transformation came after his exclusion from India's Test squad for the 2025 England tour. Following that heartbreak, Sarfaraz and his family took a drastic approach to fixing what had become a recurring problem.

His father and coach, Naushad Khan, told Hindustan Times that the household eliminated roti, rice, sugar and bakery items from Sarfaraz's diet, replacing them with grilled fish, chicken, boiled eggs, broccoli and green tea. Even the occasional biryanis that might have served as treat meals were taken off the menu entirely.

The result was a loss of around 10 kilograms within just six weeks, with the overall transformation reaching 17 kilograms over a little more than two months.

The fitness work paid immediate dividends on the field. Sarfaraz was sensational in the Syed Mushtaq Ali Trophy 2025 and smashed 329 runs at a strike rate of over 203, including three fifties and his maiden T20 century.

He also set a new record in the Vijay Hazare Trophy, producing the fastest List A half-century by any Indian batter, off just 15 deliveries. In January 2026, he hammered an unbeaten 227 in the Ranji Trophy to add a fifth first-class double hundred to his name.

MORE: Sarfaraz Khan joins rare club: Batters with First-Class 200, List A 250 and T20 century in one season

Two successive IPL auctions had passed without a buyer, with concerns over his weight management hanging over every bid.

But the transformation changed that narrative. Chennai Super Kings picked him up at his base price of Rs 75 lakh in December 2025 after his stunning Syed Mushtaq Ali Trophy campaign caught their attention.

His IPL debut produced an early highlight when he walked in at number four against Royal Challengers Bengaluru on April 5, with CSK wobbling at 30 for 3 in response to RCB's imposing 250 for 3.

Sarfaraz produced a fearless 50 off 25 balls, launching eight fours and two sixes before being stumped by Jitesh Sharma off Krunal Pandya. Even CSK captain Ruturaj Gaikwad acknowledged the knock as a gutsy effort.

MORE: Sarfaraz Khan asked to bat at No. 3 or get dropped? Report reveals shocking reason for Mumbai star’s India Test team exclusion

After that early promise, Sarfaraz couldn't sustain the momentum through the rest of IPL 2026, finishing with 160 runs from eight innings at an average of 23 and a strike rate of 169.7.

But the fitness picture heading into the next phase of his career looks better than ever, and if the latest transformation images are anything to go by, Sarfaraz is not done making statements yet.
